derivations.title: Refraction of a Plane Wave using Huygens' Principle (Snell's Law)
derivations.priority_reason: Top priority. One of the most frequently asked 3-mark questions in the board exam history.
derivations.diagram_description: Plane wavefront AB incident on interface PP' separating medium 1 (v1) and medium 2 (v2). Ray 1 hits A at t=0. Ray 2 hits C at t=tau. Secondary wavelet from A travels distance v2*tau to E in medium 2. Tangent CE represents refracted wavefront. Normals drawn to show angle i and r.

given_assumptions: Plane wavefront AB incident at angle $i$ on interface.
Speed of light in medium 1 is $v_1$, in medium 2 is $v_2$.
Time taken for wavefront to travel from B to C is $\tau$.
derivation.required: Proof of Snell's Law: $n_1 \sin i = n_2 \sin r$.

steps.text: Distance $BC = v_1 \tau$. During this time, secondary wavelet from A travels distance $AE = v_2 \tau$ in medium 2.
Consider right-angled triangle $\Delta ABC$. Angle $\angle BAC = i$.
Consider right-angled triangle $\Delta AEC$. Angle $\angle ACE = r$.
Divide $\sin i$ by $\sin r$:
By definition of refractive index $n = c/v$, so $v_1/v_2 = n_2/n_1$.
steps.latex: \frac{\sin i}{\sin r} = \frac{n_2}{n_1} \implies n_1 \sin i = n_2 \sin r

examiner_notes: Diagram must show the common hypotenuse AC.
Must explicitly label incident wavefront (AB) and refracted wavefront (CE).
Confusion between v1 and v2 leads to inverted Snell's law.
question_forms: Verify Snell's law of refraction using Huygens' principle.
Use Huygens' principle to show how a plane wavefront propagates from a rarer to a denser medium.

derivations.title: Reflection of a Plane Wave using Huygens' Principle
derivations.priority_reason: High probability alternative to the Refraction derivation. 3 Marks.
derivations.diagram_description: Plane wavefront AB incident on reflecting surface MN. Ray 1 touches A at t=0. Ray 2 touches C at t=tau. Secondary wavelet from A expands in same medium with speed v. Reflected wavefront CD is tangent to wavelet from A. Triangles ABC and ADC shown.

given_assumptions: Wave speed $v$ is same for incident and reflected waves.
derivation.required: Proof that Angle of Incidence $i$ = Angle of Reflection $r$.

steps.text: In time $\tau$, ray at B reaches C. Distance $BC = v\tau$.
In the same time, secondary wavelet from A grows to radius $AD = v\tau$.
Compare right-angled triangles $\Delta ABC$ and $\Delta ADC$. Side AC is common. Side $BC = AD = v\tau$. Angles at B and D are $90^\circ$.
Therefore, corresponding angles are equal: $\angle BAC = \angle DCA$.

examiner_notes: Students often fail to mark the 90 degree angles between ray and wavefront.
Congruence condition (RHS or SAS) should be mentally checked.
Diagram must clearly show i and r.
question_forms: Verify the law of reflection using Huygens' principle.
Show that angle of incidence equals angle of reflection using wave theory.

derivations.title: Conditions for Constructive and Destructive Interference (Analytical Treatment)
derivations.priority_reason: Mathematical foundation for YDSE. Often asked as 'Derive the expression for intensity at a point...'.
derivations.diagram_description: None required, but phasor diagram is helpful.

given_assumptions: Two sources $S_1, S_2$ with displacements $y_1 = a \cos \omega t$ and $y_2 = a \cos(\omega t + \phi)$.
derivation.required: Resultant Intensity $I$ and conditions for Max/Min.

steps.text: By principle of superposition, $y = y_1 + y_2$.
Using trigonometric identity $\cos A + \cos B = 2 \cos\frac{A+B}{2} \cos\frac{A-B}{2}$:
Resultant amplitude is $A = 2a \cos(\phi/2)$. Intensity $I \propto A^2$.
Constructive Interference (Maxima): $\cos^2(\phi/2) = 1 \implies \phi = 2n\pi$.
Destructive Interference (Minima): $\cos^2(\phi/2) = 0 \implies \phi = (2n+1)\pi$.

examiner_notes: Don't confuse Phase Difference (phi) with Path Difference (x). Relation: phi = (2pi/lambda) * x.
Must list the conditions for n (0, 1, 2...) clearly.
question_forms: Derive the expression for the intensity at a point in the interference pattern.
Obtain the conditions for constructive and destructive interference.

derivations.title: Fringe Width in Young's Double Slit Experiment (YDSE)
derivations.priority_reason: The defining derivation of Wave Optics. 5 Marks.
derivations.diagram_description: Two slits S1 and S2 separated by distance d. Screen at distance D. Central point O on screen. Point P at distance x from O. Light rays S1P and S2P meet at P. Perpendicular S1M dropped on S2P to show path difference S2M.

given_assumptions: Point P at distance $x$ from center.

steps.text: Path difference $\Delta x = S_2P - S_1P$. From geometry ($S_2P^2 - S_1P^2$):
So, $(S_2P - S_1P)(S_2P + S_1P) = 2xd$. Since $D \gg d$, $S_2P \approx S_1P \approx D$.
For Bright Fringes (Maxima): $\Delta x = n\lambda$. Position $x_n$:
For Dark Fringes (Minima): $\Delta x = (n + \frac{1}{2})\lambda$. Position $x'_n$:
Fringe Width $\beta$ is distance between consecutive bright (or dark) fringes:

examiner_notes: Approximation S2P + S1P = 2D is the key step.
Geometry method (S2P^2 - S1P^2) is preferred over small angle theta approximation in CBSE.
Must state that width is independent of n (fringes are equally spaced).
question_forms: Derive the expression for the fringe width in Young's double slit experiment.
Show that the dark and bright fringes in YDSE are equally spaced.

derivations.title: Diffraction at a Single Slit (Conditions for Minima)
derivations.priority_reason: Standard 3-mark derivation. Often confused with YDSE.
derivations.diagram_description: Single slit LN of width 'a'. Parallel beam incident. Rays diffracted at angle theta meet at P on screen. Path difference between top (L) and bottom (N) ray is NP = a sin(theta). Slit divided into zones.

steps.text: Total path difference between edges of slit is $\Delta = a \sin\theta$.
For 1st Minimum: Let $a \sin\theta = \lambda$. Divide slit into 2 halves. Path difference between corresponding points in upper and lower half is $\lambda/2$. They cancel out.
For 2nd Minimum: Let $a \sin\theta = 2\lambda$. Divide slit into 4 quarters. Path diff between quarters is $\lambda/2$. They cancel in pairs.
Generalizing for $n^{th}$ minimum:

examiner_notes: Major Confusion: For Diffraction, minima is n*lambda. For Interference, maxima is n*lambda. Do not mix them.
Must explain the 'dividing slit into halves' logic to get marks.
question_forms: Derive the condition for secondary minima in single slit diffraction.
Show that the angular width of the central maximum is 2lambda/a.

derivations.title: Refraction at Brewster's Angle (Polarization)
derivations.priority_reason: Simple derivation, often asked if Polarization is in syllabus. Checks basic geometry.
derivations.diagram_description: Incident ray at angle ip. Reflected ray and Refracted ray are perpendicular (90 degrees).

given_assumptions: Reflected and refracted rays are perpendicular.

steps.text: From diagram, angle of reflection is $i_p$. Angle of refraction is $r$. Since rays are perpendicular:
From Snell's Law:
Substitute $r$:

examiner_notes: State clearly: Reflected and refracted rays are perpendicular at polarizing angle.
question_forms: Show that at polarizing angle, the reflected and refracted rays are perpendicular to each other.
